spec_match.rationale: The builder now evaluates the exact single-argument CSS.supports() condition text, uses the spec's identifier form for named-feature(), and emits valid @supports code for bare declarations.

interactive.rationale: Chrome DevTools MCP testing exercised the default query, or, not B, custom bare declarations, an unsupported declaration, and the Evaluate button with live readouts and card state changes.

blurb_alignment.rationale: The concept and feature index copy now describe named-feature() without quoted string syntax and match the compound-condition behavior shown in the UI.

edge_cases.rationale: Parenthesized declarations, bare declaration input, unsupported declarations, and unsupported Chrome 150 named-feature fallback all produce explicit results without rewriting the tested condition text.

references.rationale: The page cites the ChromeStatus entry and CSS Conditional Rules Level 5 specification.

design_system.rationale: The touched UI uses shared design variables, local classes instead of inline styles, and clear supported/unsupported contrast states.

summary: Verified in Chrome 148 via chrome-devtools-mcp at /v150/named-feature-function-for-css-supports/compound-condition/: the default named-feature(anchor-position-follows-transforms) fallback is clean, (anchor-name: --x) remains supported, or applies enhanced styling, not B generates and not (display: grid), bare declarations are wrapped in the generated @supports CSS, and unknown-prop: nope is negated correctly. The feature conformance route reported 8 pass and 0 fail with clean console and network logs.
